message.content:
POLARIS COMPOSE — THE INVESTIGATION.

WHAT COMPOSE DOES: renders 346 baskets into a deep research report. Compose model = z-ai/glm-5.2 via
OpenRouter. Each rendered sentence is verified by an NLI entailment judge (also via OpenRouter, SAME
account). Goal: render ALL 346 baskets fast. Today it is far too slow.

WORKTREE (read here): /home/polaris/wt/compose   — also /workspace/POLARIS for reference
KEY FILES: src/polaris_graph/generator/abstractive_writer.py, generator/quantified_analysis.py,
  the NLI/entailment judge + provenance_generator, scripts/run_s5_i3.py, launch_compose_gear_*.sh

WHAT IS ALREADY KNOWN — DO NOT RE-FIND THIS, GO PAST IT:
Commit 0615bc5 fixed ONE serialization bug: _draft_passes_wrapper (abstractive_writer.py:661) was a
sync `def` called with NO `await` from the async coroutine _pre_pass_one_basket (:749), at two call
sites. It ran writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> entailment_judge.judge() ->
_post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(timeout=...) which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD — and the calling
thread was the asyncio event loop. So every NLI judge POST froze the whole compose loop and achieved
concurrency was 1, regardless of PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16.
Both call sites now `await asyncio.to_thread(...)`. Measured A/B: 2.06s -> 0.60s on 4 baskets.

THE OPERATOR'S QUESTION, AND THE POINT OF THIS INVESTIGATION:
"The 48-way parallelism was never real. One missing await is a suspiciously tidy explanation. What
ELSE is capping compose? Is the context too large? Not enough token budget? Crashes? Something else?"
Your job is to find the REMAINING throughput killers. Assume there are more. There usually are.

RULES OF EVIDENCE (this is the whole game):
- A finding is a MECHANISM at file:line, not a vibe. "Context might be big" is worthless.
  "prompt is built at :412 by concatenating all N basket digests with no cap, so a 346-basket run
  sends ~X tokens per call, and TTFT scales linearly with it" is a finding.
- READ THE ACTUAL CODE. Do not reason from the summary above. Open the files.
- Where you can, PROVE it — a tiny probe script in /tmp, a python -c, a log grep with counts.
  A measured number beats a code-reading every time.
- Report REAL output. If you find nothing in your lane, say "nothing found" — a clean lane is a
  useful result. Do NOT invent a finding to look productive.
- NEVER print the OpenRouter API key.


YOU ARE FABLE. Eight independent Opus investigators read the compose path line-by-line. Every finding
was then attacked by an adversarial refuter. These are the ones that SURVIVED:

It does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` then `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` — a blocking wait for a FULL GLM-5.2 writer call. Because `_compose_section_per_basket` itself runs on the event loop (finding 1), this `fut.result()` blocks the event loop for the entire writer call, bounded only at call_deadline_s+30 = 300+30 = 330 SECONDS. It fires up to PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX (default 2) times per basket, for every basket whose pre-pass draft failed verification. The function's own docstring states it 'runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop'.",

      "reason": "The mechanism is real, reachable, and blocks the event loop — I could not refute it.\n\nCONFIRMED by reading the actual code:\n1. Call site runs ON the loop. `_compose_section_per_basket` is invoked as a plain synchronous call (no `await`, no `to_thread`) at multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514, inside `async def _run_section` (:5182). I verified there is no thread offload wrapping it. The pre-pass above it (:5448 `await abstractive_pre_pass`) is properly awaited, but the compose call is not.\n2. The redraft path is LIVE under iter5. launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh sets PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1; `_vc_redraft_fn = _make_group_redraft_fn(...)` is non-None when `_synth_primary_active` (:5464); it is threaded into `_compose_section_per_basket(redraft_fn=...)` (:5475).\n3. The blocking wait is real. `_synth_primary_repair_loop` (verified_compose.py:2136) calls `fresh = redraft_fn(...)` synchronously; `_redraft` (multi_section_generator.py:4969) does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` then `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` (:4994-4996). `fut.result()` blocks the CALLING thread, which is the event-loop thread. The function's own docstring (:4938-4940) explicitly states it \"runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop\" and uses a ThreadPoolExecutor precisely BECAUSE `asyncio.run` cannot nest in a running loop — the design ACKNOWLEDGES it blocks the loop.\n4. Blocking the loop actually costs concurrency. `_gather_sections_isolated` (verified_compose... msg:1003-1005) drives all sections via `asyncio.ensure_future` + `asyncio.gather`, so multiple `_run_section` coroutines share one loop. Any synchronous block in one section freezes every other section.\n\nThis is the SAME defect class as the already-fixed finding-1 (sync blocking network call executed on the event-loop thread), but on the redraft path, which the 0615bc5 fix did NOT touch.\n\nKill attempts that FAILED: (a) no `to_thread`/thread offload wraps the compose call; (b) the design deliberately runs it on the loop; (c) sections are genuinely concurrent so the block has a cost.\n\nHonest caveats (which limit magnitude but do not refute the mechanism): redraft fires ONLY for baskets whose pre-pass draft had ≥1 sentence FAIL verification (not all 346), so frequency depends on the verify-failure rate, which I could not measure — the I-rdy-018 live logs showed 0 firings but predate/don't exercise this slate. But those bound the impact, they do not make the claim false.",

      "real_impact": "Each redraft is a LIVE GLM-5.2 group-writer call (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=131072, REASONING_MAX_TOKENS=65536, CALL_DEADLINE_S=300) run via fut.result() on the event-loop thread, firing up to PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX=2 times per basket whose pre-pass draft failed verification. While it blocks, the whole loop — every concurrently-gathered section — is frozen. Worst-case block per call = call_deadline_s+30 = 330s (only on a wedge); typical block = one full writer-call latency, realistically seconds to tens of seconds given the 65536-token reasoning budget (NOT the ~0.6s cached pre-pass latency, because redraft is a fresh network call, not a dict lookup). At 346-basket scale, if even ~15-20% of baskets fail verification (~50-70 baskets) × up to 2 redrafts × ~10-30s each = hundreds to >1000s of pure serialized event-loop blocking during which zero cross-section parallelism exists. The \"330s per basket\" figure is the true upper bound (the timeout wall), not the expected per-basket cost. Fix mirrors finding-1: wrap the redraft invocation in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` (or make the repair loop / compose async), so the writer call runs off the loop."
    }

(2) The path is reached at runtime: default `_entailment_mode()` is 'enforce' (strict_verify.py:369, \"I-bug-095: default mode is 'enforce'\"), so every writer-drafted sentence hits `_get_judge().judge()` (strict_verify.py:516) → `_post_with_total_deadline` → `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155) before the POST. The cap is 4 concurrent (measured =4), not 1. Serialization-to-1 is what finding 1 (the missing await) caused; this is a 4-way cap = a ~3x throttle at writer=12, not full serialization. (b) The claim's consequence (b) — \"the thread doing sem.acquire() IS the event loop, so slot-queueing is loop-freeze\" — is ALREADY VOID. The 0615bc5 fix wrapped `_draft_passes_wrapper` in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` at abstractive_writer.py:833 and :912, so acquire() now runs on a worker thread, never the loop. Only consequence (a) survives. Since the central, load-bearing claim (a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) caps in-flight NLI POSTs below the intended writer parallelism and thereby caps the pre-pass speedup) is true and reached and material, refuted=FALSE.",

      "real_impact": "Judge concurrency is hard-capped at 4 in-flight process-wide, versus PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 (iter5) or =24 (iter3/iter4). The verify leg is the majority of per-basket POSTs: each basket does 1 writer LLM call + S sequential per-sentence judge POSTs (S~3-5; _draft_passes_wrapper loops sentences serially, so one basket uses at most 1 slot at a time — the >4 demand materializes when >4 baskets are simultaneously in their verify phase, which holds most of the time since verify is the longer phase). Effective judge throughput is therefore 4/12 = 33% of intended at iter5 (~3x throttle), and 4/24 = ~6x throttle at iter3/iter4. At 346-basket scale that is ~1000-1700 judge POSTs; each is a high-effort glm-5.2 NLI call (code comments cite ~6-40s observed). Order-of-magnitude: ~1400 posts x ~10s / 4 ≈ 58 min for the verify leg vs / 12 ≈ 19 min — i.e. the 4-cap adds on the order of tens of minutes to a full run, and it caps the 0615bc5 pre-pass speedup regardless of the 12/24 writer setting. Cheap fix: set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to match the writer concurrency (12/24) in the launch scripts — the de-storm rationale (avoiding a 32-simultaneous 429 burst) is satisfied at 12 spread over 4 provider hosts (~3/host) and the slot is only held for the POST, not retry backoffs."

      "reason": "The claim survives every refutation attempt. All mechanism points check out against the real code:\n\n1. The default asyncio executor cap (min(32,cpu+4)) sits ABOVE 4, so the semaphore remains the tighter cap.       "real_impact": "A hard process-wide 4-way cap on ALL NLI verdicts during compose, independent of PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY (12-24) and PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS (2-3). content.text:
Spot-checks confirm the two load-bearing facts at HEAD in /home/polaris/wt/compose: `_compose_section_per_basket` is still called bare (no await/to_thread) at src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514, and `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4` at src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:55 with `PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY` set in zero launch scripts (grep exit 1).

WHY COMPOSE IS SLOW — SYNTHESIS OF SURVIVING FINDINGS

1. RANKED CAUSES (real cost at 346-basket scale)

#1 — The compose phase itself still runs ON the event loop. [MEASURED, dominant]
src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514: `async def _run_section` calls the sync `_compose_section_per_basket` (verified_compose.py:3262) with no await and no to_thread. Inside it, every per-sentence verify blocks on `fut.result()` and — worse — the SYNTH_PRIMARY redraft (`_make_group_redraft_fn`, multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996) blocks the loop on a FULL glm-5.2 writer call, up to 330s per firing, up to 2x per failed basket. 0615bc5 fixed this bug class in the pre-pass only; this is the same bug one layer up, unfixed at HEAD.
Measured: heartbeat probe showed the loop did not tick for 631 consecutive seconds during one section's compose (86% of a 734s section); instrumented section 0: judge busy 673.0s of 735.2s wall at inflight pinned to 1; iter4 log: section 3 drafted 0/13 baskets in 1700s while section 2 held the loop, then 13/13 in 315s once released; section 0 total 3266s of which only 591s (pre-pass) was parallel — 82% of the section at concurrency 1. This is the reason PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16, and PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS are all inert during compose. Cost: the majority of the measured ~7.4h iter4 wall.

#2 — Process-global NLI judge semaphore hard-capped at 4. [MEASURED, becomes binding the moment #1 is fixed]
judge_concurrency.py:55 DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4; entailment_judge.py:155 wraps every judge POST in `_acquire_judge_slot()`; PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is set in no launcher (verified again just now). The judge uses its own httpx client, so PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 never applies to it. Measured: probes admit peak exactly 4 with 24/48 waiters (6.0x slowdown vs unbounded); a real ledger session ran 2170 judge calls at achieved concurrency 3.94 for 8208s. At ~4.4-5.5s/verdict and ~1000-2000 verdicts per 346-basket run, cap 4 puts a ~19-46 min floor under the verify leg; cap 12 cuts that ~3x. Today it is masked by #1 (a serial caller never needs more than 1 slot).

#3 — sentence_repair: awaited one-at-a-time repair loop + sync verify on the loop. [MEASURED counts, MEDIUM]
sentence_repair.py:408/:428/:519. ~90-99 repair attempts per run across ~10-11 sections, each a serialized glm-5.2 reasoning call plus a loop-blocking NLI POST. Bounded per-section (cap 10), does not scale with baskets; order of 1-2 min of loop freeze plus serialized repair tails. Fix after #1/#2.

#4 — coherence_rewrite_section sync verify in async loop. [CODE-READ only, LOW]
section_polish.py:271-283. Real bug, same class, but fires only on changed sentences, once per section — tens of seconds per run at most. Refuter downgraded from HIGH.

NOT a cause — 429s / API errors. [MEASURED] 12 of 4639 POSTs were 429 (0.26%), all recovered, 180s total backoff = 0.68% of wall. The original "429 is the ceiling" thesis remains dead — with one caveat under gap (5).

2. THE OPERATOR'S THREE HYPOTHESES, BY NAME

- CONTEXT TOO LARGE? NO, on current evidence. Judge latency measured flat across span sizes (median 4.4-5.5s); single writer calls measure 7-11s live. Nothing in eight lanes surfaced an unbounded prompt-concatenation mechanism. Caveat: nobody actually counted prompt tokens per call — this is a "no confirmed mechanism + flat latency" no, not a token-count no.
- TOKEN BUDGET? NO — the opposite. No truncation finding survived. Budgets are enormous (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=131072, reasoning 65536), which makes each *serialized* redraft call take 30-78s. Budget is not starving compose; oversized reasoning budgets amplify the serialization cost of #1.
- CRASHES? NO. Zero errors/exceptions in the measured windows; all 12 429s retried to 200; no abandonments except the pre-pass wall-deadline event, which was itself CAUSED by #1 (a starved sibling section), not a crash.

The real answer to "what else is capping compose" is: the same missing-await bug class, twice more (compose phase and its redraft path), plus a 4-slot judge semaphore inherited from a different workload.

3. THE ONE THING
Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` at multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514 (functools.partial for kwargs). This unfreezes the loop: sections genuinely overlap, sibling pre-passes stop starving, and the redraft's inner `asyncio.run` (already on its own worker thread) becomes correct off-loop. Expected speedup: the measured runs spent ~82-86% of section wall at concurrency 1 because of this; restoring 2-3-way section overlap plus intra-run pipelining is a realistic 3-6x on total wall. Strongly recommended rider (one line, zero code): export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=12 in the launcher — otherwise the to_thread fix immediately hits the 4-slot judge wall and you capture only part of the win.

4. HONEST WALL-CLOCK AFTER THE TOP FIX
Baseline: 346 baskets took ~7.4h (iter4). With the to_thread fix ALONE: verify floor = ~1000-2000 judge POSTs x ~5s / 4 slots ≈ 21-42 min, plus writer/redraft legs at 12-16 wide ≈ 10-25 min, partially pipelined — estimate ~1 to 1.5 hours. With the judge cap also raised to 12: verify floor drops to ~7-15 min — estimate ~30-60 minutes. This rests on four assumptions: (a) judge-POST density ~3-5/basket holds across all 346 baskets (measured on sections 0-3 only); (b) the redraft/verify-failure rate stays at observed levels (not measured at full scale); (c) no 429 storm at true 12-16-way concurrency (429s were only measured under serialization — see gaps); (d) per-call latencies (judge ~5s, writer 7-30s) hold. Treat 30-90 min as the honest band, not a point estimate.

5. WHAT WE DID NOT ESTABLISH
- Verify-failure/redraft frequency at 346-basket scale. The 330s-per-redraft path is confirmed live, but how many of 346 baskets trigger it is unmeasured; it swings the estimate by tens of minutes.
- 429 behavior at real concurrency. "429 is not a cap" was measured at ~0.17 req/s effective — i.e., while serialized. At genuine 12-16-way on one OpenRouter account it may bind; must be re-measured after the fix.
- The verdict-cache contradiction. One measured lane saw 105 real phase-2 judge POSTs (cache misses); another verdict says compose-phase verifies are cache hits from the pre-pass. The cache-key mismatch (span re-resolution?) is unexplained; it determines whether phase-2 verify cost is ~0 or ~600s/section post-fix.
- Writer-vs-judge dominance post-fix. Whether generation (16-way pool) or verify (judge pool) is the binding stage after both fixes is unresolved; they may co-bottleneck.
- Actual prompt token counts. The "context not too large" answer is inferential; no one measured tokens-per-call across basket sizes.
- No end-to-end A/B of the fixed build at 346 baskets exists yet. Everything above extrapolates from 4-15-basket probes and the iter4 log.
